coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorometric epinephrine and norepinephrine assay based on carbon dot and CdTe quantum dots nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yifan
Wang, Bei
Xiong, Huayu
Wen, Wei
Cheng, Nan
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe, consisted of water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and 3‑mercapropionic acid-coated cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs), was constructed for the detection of epinephrine (EP) and norepinephrine (NE). Under single-wavelength excitation (370 nm), the hybrid nanoprobe possessed dual emission peaks at 460 and 610 nm belongs to CDs and CdTe QDs, respectively. CdTe QDs fluorescence was quenched by EP or NE via electron transfer from QDs to the oxidation products of catecholamines, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained almost constant, which provided a ratiometric fluorescence assay for the catecholamines detection. The relative fluorescence intensity ratios were directly proportional to the concentration of catecholamines. Meanwhile, some structural analogues of the target molecules, including dopamine hydrochloride and isoprenaline hydrochloride, had no significant interference for the detection mode. The novel fluorescence detection platform was rapid and convenient, and applied to the detection of EP and NE in human serum samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorometric
Epinephrine
Norepinephrine
Carbon dots
Quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CDs@SiO2@QDs hybrid spheres
Quenching effect of EP and NE on CDs@SiO2@QDs
Selectivity of CDs@SiO2@QDs nanoprobe for the detection of EP and NE
Determination of EP and NE in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) CDs, (B) CDs@SiO2, (C) CdTe QDs and (D) CDs@SiO2@QDs.
FT-IR spectra of AEAPMS, CDs and CdTe QDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@SiO2@QDs (a) CDs@SiO2@QDs- EP (b) and CDs@SiO2@QDs-NE (c), (B) fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs@SiO2 (a) and CdTe QDs (b), (C) emission spectra of CDs@SiO2 (a), CdTe QDs (b) and CDs@SiO2@QDs (c) and (D) fluorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2 (a), CDs@SiO2 with the addition of 40 μM EP (b) and CDs@SiO2 with the addition of 20 μM NE (c). (E) Fluorescence spectra of QDs (a), QDs with the addition of 40 μM EP (b) and QDs with the addition of 20 μM NE (c). (F) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2@QDs (a), CDs@SiO2@QDs with the addition of 40 μM EP (b) and CDs@SiO2@QDs with the addition of 20 μM NE (c).

(A, C) The fluorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2@QDs upon the addition of different concentration of (A) EP in the range of 0 to 40 μM (0, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 40 μM), (C)NE in the range of 0 to 20 μM (0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 8, 10, 20 μM). (B, D) The fluorescence emission intensity ratio F610nm/F460nm of CDs@SiO2@QDs versus the concentration of (B)EP and (D)NE (λex = 370 nm).

The fluorescence intensity ratio F610nm/F460nm of CDs@SiO2@QDs (A) in the presence of 500 μM of DA, AA, UA, DOPAC, ISO, 5-HT, and 10 μM of EP, (B) both EP (10 μM) and other interfering substances, (C) in the presence of 500 μM of DA, AA, UA, DOPAC, ISO, 5-HT, and 10 μM of NE and (D) both NE (10 μM) and other interfering substances.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication procedures and the designed principle for ratiometric fluorescence detection of EP and NE based on CDs@SiO2@QDs (λex = 370 nm, λem = 460 nm/610 nm).
Results for the determination of EP and NE in spiked human serum samples.
Comparison of the analytical methods for the detection of EP and NE.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorometric epinephrine and norepinephrine assay based on carbon dot and CdTe quantum dots nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, College of Food Science & Nutritional Engineering, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health
College of Food Science & Nutritional Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, Hubei Normal University

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: H. Xiong, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe, consisted of water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and 3‑mercapropionic acid-coated cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs), was constructed for the detection of epinephrine (EP) and norepinephrine (NE). Under single-wavelength excitation (370 nm), the hybrid nanoprobe possessed dual emission peaks at 460 and 610 nm belongs to CDs and CdTe QDs, respectively. CdTe QDs fluorescence was quenched by EP or NE via electron transfer from QDs to the oxidation products of catecholamines, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained almost constant, which provided a ratiometric fluorescence assay for the catecholamines detection. The relative fluorescence intensity ratios were directly proportional to the concentration of catecholamines. Meanwhile, some structural analogues of the target molecules, including dopamine hydrochloride and isoprenaline hydrochloride, had no significant interference for the detection mode. The novel fluorescence detection platform was rapid and convenient, and applied to the detection of EP and NE in human serum samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Catecholamines (e.g., epinephrine (EP) and norepinephrine (NE)) play important roles as neurotransmitters or hormones in mammalian central nervous and cardiovascular systems [1]. Their abnormal concentration can be related to the variety of diseases including Schizophrenia, Parkinsonism, Alzheimer, severe head trauma, pheochromocytoma, various neuroblastoma and depression [2,3]. Hence, sensitive analysis of catecholamine's abnormalities is valuable for early diseases diagnosis and pathological study. For the detection of catecholamines, several techniques including spectrophotometry [4], chemiluminescence [5], electrochemistry [6], capillary electrophoresis [7], and high-performance liquid chromatography [8,9], single wavelength fluorescence intensity modulation type sensors (turn-on or turn-off sensors) [1,10] have been developed. However, due to their low concentration, easy oxidation and similar structures, some of the approaches have shortcomings in terms of sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and complexity [11]. Therefore, developing simple, selective and sensitive method for the detection of NE and EP is urgently needed.

Recently, the ratiometric fluorescence probe, which employs the ratio of two emission intensities at different wavelengths, has attracted increasing attention owing to its improved sensitivity at trace quantity levels of analytes [12,13]. The use of the ratio of two interconnected fluorescence signals as the detection index could effectively eliminate the background interference and the fluctuation of detection conditions [14]. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention in various fields owing to their unique properties, such as aqueous solubility, flexibility in surface modification, high photostability, and tunable photoluminescence [15–17]. Usually, the abundant surface oxygen-containing functional groups were existed in CDs, which have been recognized as a promising support for construction of optical sensors for quantitative assay [18]. For example, in order to improve the measurement accuracy and sensitivity, CDs-based ratiometric assays have been developed by using CDs as a built-in correction for external interference [19–21]. Lu et al. have developed a ratiometric phosphorescence nanoprobe for thallium detection based on Mn-doped ZnSe quantum dots (QDs) and C-dots. In the presence of thallium, the emission from Mn-doped ZnSe QDs could be selectively quenched, while that from C-dots retained unchanged [21].

Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) exhibit superior optical properties with tunable absorption/emission profiles, high photo-stability and different functional groups on their surfaces through easily synthesis. Because of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer or electron transfer processes stimulated by excited QDs, the applications have been focused on the biorecognition or biocatalytic reactions [22–24]. The surface states and modifications of QDs have effect not only on the luminescence properties, but also to enable interaction with other substances. Therefore, it is reasonable to expect the chemical or physical interaction between the surface functional groups of nanoparticles and some given chemical species, which would result in changes in the efficiency of the core electron-hole recombination, thus in sensing capability of QDs [25–27]. Hu and coworkers [28] induced high selectivity and binding affinity towards EP against NE and serotonin by using molecularly imprinted polymer strategy based on CdTe@SiO2 quantum dots. Ghasemi et al. [1] have discriminated catecholamine neurotransmitters based on the combination of the fluorescence properties of their oxidation products under alkaline conditions and the fluorescence quenching on thioglycolic acid functionalized CdTe QDs by the oxidation products. But the fluorescence of their oxidation products decreased very fast due to the formation of polymeric products in complicated pathway of catecholamine oxidation under alkaline conditions, which cannot be used for accurate quantification.

Herein, we describe a CDs@SiO2@QDs-based ratiometric catecholamines assay in aqueous solution. For the fabrication of the detection system, firstly we synthesized SiO2-coated carbon dots (CDs@SiO2), then the probe CdTe QDs for catecholamines was covalently coupled onto the surface of SiO2. CDs were embedded in the silica microsphere to serve as a signal reference source, and CdTe QDs were covalently coated on the surface of the silica microsphere as a signal response source which could be quenched by catecholamines based on the electron transfer (ET) from QDs to quinones (the oxidation products of catecholamines) (Scheme 1 ).

N‑(β‑aminoethyl)‑γ‑aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3‑aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), 3‑mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA), petroleum ether, 1‑ethyl‑3‑(3‑dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), N‑hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), citric acid anhydrous (CA), cadmium chloride hemi (pentahydrate) (CdCl2·5/2H2O), sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), NH3·H2O were purchased from Aladdin company (Shanghai, China). EP, NE, dopamine hydrochloride (DA·HCl), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), 3,4‑dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC), isoprenaline hydrochloride (ISO·HCl), and 5‑serotonin (5-HT) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). NaOH and ethanol were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (Mill-Q, Millipore).

The morphology of nanoparticles were characterized by a Tecnai G20 (FEI Co., Holland) transmission electron microscope (TEM) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The fluorescence measurements were performed with a RF-5301PC fluorometry (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1 in KBr were recorded using Spectrum One (PerkinElmer, American).The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired using a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrometer (, Shimadzu, Japan).

CDs were synthesized through decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid with the surface passivation reaction of the amine groups of AEAPMS according to the previously reported one-pot synthesis procedures [29]. First, 10 mL AEAPMS were placed into a 50 mL three-necked flask, and degassed with nitrogen for 10 min. After that the solution was heated till the temperature reached 240 °C. Keeping at that temperature, 0.5 g CA as carbon source was quickly added to the solution with vigorous stirring for 1 min under nitrogen condition. The final products were purified with petroleum ether three times, and then the obtained 2 mL of CDs were dispersed in 35 mL of ethanol solution for use.

CDs@SiO2 was synthesized using previously reported procedures [30] with minor modifications. 35 mL of the prepared CDs solution, 310 μL of APTES (functional monomer) and 1.4 mL of TEOS (cross-linker) were successively added into a 50 mL flask under stirring. After that the mixture of 120 μL ammonia solution and 860 μL water was added into the reaction flask. The mixture was allowed to proceed for 24 h to induce hydrolyzation and co-condensation of the CDs with the added silica precursors at room temperature. The resulting product was collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with ethanol to discard the reagents. CDs@SiO2 were dried in a vacuum chamber at 60 °C overnight, and then dissolved in ethanol-water mixture (1:1, v/v) for further experiments. The final concentration of CDs@SiO2 was 0.05 mg/mL.

Water-soluble carboxyl-functionalized CdTe QDs were synthesized as previously described [31] with minor modifications. Briefly, 14.3 mg (3.31 mM) of CdCl2·5/2 H2O and 8.0 mg (7.5 mM) 3-MPA was mixed in 10 ml deionized water, and the pH was adjusted to 8.0 by dropwise addition of NaOH solution with stirring. And then the mixture of 2.8 mg (1.25 mM) of Na2TeO3 and 1.2 mg (3.125 mM) of NaBH4 was added into the reaction flask. Then, the system was placed in reaction kettle and heated to 100 °C for 1 h. Water-compatible 3-MPA-capped CdTe QDs was purified three times by isopropyl alcohol with centrifugation at 10000 rpm and stored in the dark at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CDs@SiO2@QDs hybrid spheres
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs was attached to CDs@SiO2 by following the protocol: The entire process consists of activation of CdTe QDs followed by addition of CDs@SiO2 to the activated CdTe QDs. 1 mL of EDC/NHS (0.5 mg∙mL−1for each) was added into 2 mL of CdTe QDs (0.05 mg∙mL−1) and the reaction mixture was allowed to stir for 15 min in dark. Then, 2 mL of CDs@SiO2 (0.05 mg∙mL−1) dispersion was added to it, and the reaction mixture was allowed to stir for 0.5 h in the dark. The resultant CDs@SiO2@QDs hybrid spheres were collected by centrifugation and washing, which was re-dispersed in 10 mL of water for later use.

The freshly EP or NE stock solution was prepared by dissolving EP or NE in NaOH solution (pH 8.5). Afterwards, the standard solution was prepared by adding different volumes of stock solution to a microcentrifuge tube and diluting with deionized water to 1 mL. CDs@SiO2@QDs were dispersed in appropriate volume of ultrapure water by ultrasonic vibration, with a final concentration as 0.02 mg∙mL−1. For detection, 1 mL of the standard solution containing EP or NE was added into 1 mL CDs@SiO2@QDs. The mixture was vortexed vigorously for 15 s, and then incubated at room temperature for 6 and 15 min for EP and NE, respectively. After that, the mixtures were scanned with a fluorescence spectrometer. Typically, the fluorescence measurements were performed under the following conditions: the excitation wavelength was set at 370 nm with the slit width was both 5 nm for excitation and emission.

The human serum samples were supplied by a local hospital, which were mixed with methyl alcohol and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the possibly existed protein before analysis. Various amounts of EP or NE were sequentially spiked into the supernatant of serum samples and then reacted with the CDs@SiO2@QDs. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded after the incubation.

The morphology and dispersity of the synthesized nanoparticles were studied by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 , CDs and CdTe QDs are highly uniform mono-dispersive. The diameters of CDs and CdTe QDs were about 3 and 5 nm, respectively. CDs@SiO2 had highly uniform spherical morphology with smooth surfaces, the diameter of which increased to about 25 nm. The size is much larger than that of CDs, which is probably because several CDs are encapsulated in one particle. Fig. 1D shows that some small particles had been deposited on the surface of CDs@SiO2 and thus a crude surface was generated, implying the successful formation of CDs@SiO2@QDs hybrid spheres.

It has been hypothesized that the remarkable photoluminescence of CDs is associated with passivated surface defects of the core carbon particles. The successful surface passivation reaction of CDs and capping of MPA onto CdTe QDs surface has been confirmed by comparing the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 ). Different from pure AEAPMS, FTIR spectrum of CDs showed two characteristic peaks at 1644 cm−1 and 1566 cm−1 assigning to the vibrations of amide I and amide II, respectively [30]. The two typical signals of CONR vibrations suggested the successful surface passivation reaction of CDs. FTIR spectrum of CdTe QDs exhibited two absorption peaks at around 1403 and 1562 cm−1 assigning to the symmetric and anti-symmetric stretching vibrations of the carbonyl group, virtually confirming that CdTe QDs had been capped by carboxyl group [32,33]. Furthermore, the two spectral bands centered at 1640 and 3401 cm−1 correspond to CO stretching vibration and OH stretching vibration, respectively.

The optical properties of the obtained fluorescence probe were studied in detail. UV/Vis absorption spectrum of CDs@SiO2@QDs revealed an absorption band at ~290 nm (Fig. 3A, curve a). After the addition of EP or NE, another absorption band at around 350 nm was appearance (Fig. 3A, curves b and c), which belongs to quinone formation [34,35]. Fig. 3B revealed that CDs@SiO2 (curve a) and CdTe QDs (curve b) emitted strong excitation peak at around 365 and 378 nm, respectively. The positions of the two excitation peaks are relatively close. Fig. 3C revealed that CDs@SiO2 (curve a) and CdTe QDs (curve b) emitted strong fluorescence with the maximal emission wavelengths at 460 and 600 nm when excited at around 365 and 378 nm, respectively. To match the absorption of the acceptors, we selected 370 nm as a single wavelength excitation, and the as-prepared CDs@SiO2@QDs hybrid spheres had dual emission peaks at 460 nm and 610 nm with different intensities ascribed to the respective emission of CDs and CdTe QDs, respectively (curve c, Fig. 3C). The emission for CdTe QDs was switched from 600 nm to 610 nm due to the elongation of the fluorescent system by the formation of hybrid spheres. Moreover, the wavelength difference in the emission peak is as large as 150 nm between CDs and CdTe QDs (460 and 610 nm), which can effectively exclude the interference among the two emission peaks. So the construction of CDs@SiO2@QDs is favorable for the nanoparticles to act as ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe for the recognition of objection. Moreover, CdTe QDs as signal labels were loaded on the surface of silica microspheres, instead of traditionally embedded in the silica microspheres, which can maintain the brightness and stability of the fluorescence signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of EP and NE on CDs@SiO2@QDs
ce:section.ce:para: On addition of EP or NE, the emission peak of CDs@SiO2 keeps almost the same, whereas the peak of QDs decreased a litter. By contrast, for CDs@SiO2@QDs, the emission peak appeared at 460 nm keep constant, meanwhile the emission at 610 nm decreased till disappeared, indicative of a ratiometric response. It is well known that the catecholamine could be easily oxidized to its quinone derivative under a neutral and alkaline conditions [36,37]. In this work, EP and NE were dissolved in alkaline conditions, which was useful for the oxidation of catechol group in EP and NE. A color change of solution to brownish accompanied by the absorption band at around 350 cm−1 suggested the quinone formation. Such fluorescence quenching effects could happen due to the ET from QDs to quinones. Due to the presence of OH and NH2 groups in EP, NE and silica microsphere, hydrogen bond promotes these quinones (electron acceptor compounds) approaching the surface of 3-MPA capped QDs. Consequently, significant QD quenching was observed in the presence of EP and NE. CDs were protected by silica microspheres, so the fluorescence signal retained unchanged.

The dose response of CDs@SiO2@QDs ratiometric probe to EP or NE has been examined under the optimized experimental conditions. Upon addition of various concentrations of EP (Fig. 4A) or NE (Fig. 4C), the fluorescence intensity at 610 nm decreased continuously with increasing concentrations of objections, while the intensity at 460 nm reminded constant. The quenching effect of ~95% was achieved with the concentration of EP or NE at 40 μM. As shown in Fig. 4B, a good linear relationship between F610nm/F460nm and the concentration of EP within the range of 0.01–20 μM was observed. The linear curve was fitted with the regression equation F610nm / F460nm = −0.0637c (μM) + 1.8514 (R2 = 0.9933) and a detection limit of 6.8 nM was determined at S/N = 3. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be 5.9% corresponded to six independent measurements at the EP concentration of 20 μM. Fig. 4D shows that a good linearity between the F610nm/F460nm ratio and NE concentration was also obtained when the NE concentration ranged from 0.005 to 10 μM with the regression equation F610nm / F460nm = −0.1285c (μM) + 1.8755 (R2 = 0.9965), and the limit of detection was estimated to be 2.1 nM at S/N = 3. RSD was calculated to be 3.7% corresponded to six independent measurements at the NE concentration of 20 μM. The results illustrated that the proposed nanosensor possessed excellent reproducibility. Additionally, the analytical performance of this ratiometric fluorescence probe was compared with that of other determination methods reported in the literature [1,4,5,35,38,39]. The features are summarized in Table 2. As shown, the proposed probe exhibited a wide linear range and a low detection limit for EP and NE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs@SiO2@QDs nanoprobe for the detection of EP and NE
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the selectivity of CDs@SiO2@QDs, interference of the structural analogues of the detection molecules was investigated under the optimum condition. The fluorescence responses of CDs@SiO2@QDs were measured in the presence of 10 μM EP (Fig. 5A) or 10 μM NE (Fig. 5C) and 50-fold higher levels of DA·HCl, AA, UA, DOPAC, ISO·HCl, and 5-HT. The sensor displayed a significant selective fluorescence quenching for EP and NE over other structural analogues with relative larger amounts. The fluorescence intensity ratios of the probe were also recorded (F610nm/F460nm) in the presence of EP (Fig. 5 B) or NE (Fig. 5 D) and other structural analogues. No tremendous changes were observed by adding other ions into the sensing system, indicating that the coexistence of these structural analogues has minor interloping on the good selectivity of the dual-emission NPs when exposed to EP and NE. The reason might be the fact that for DA·HCl and ISO·HCl dissolved in water, pH of those solutions were acidic, which was not suitable for the oxidation of phenol to benzoquinone in the complete absence of specific oxidants [40]. The largest disadvantage of this work is not to measure specifically EP or NE. However, both NE and EP are the same biomarkers for some diseases in clinical laboratories, and the diagnosis generally can be done by measuring NE and/or EP in 24 h urine [35]. If the goal is to measure specifically EP or NE, it should be used some published methods [1,11,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of EP and NE in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the reliability and applicability of the fluorescence method, CDs@SiO2@QDs as a fluorescence probe was used to determine EP and NE concentrations in human serum samples. The spiked samples were obtained using the serum out of proteins by adding the different known amounts of EP or NE. As can be seen from the Table 1 , the average assay recoveries of spiked EP are 95.0–104.0% with a RSD ranging from 1.5% to 5.3%, and the recoveries of spiked NE are 96.0%–108.0% with a RSD ranging from 0.3% to 2.4%. The results indicated the method could be applied to the detection of DA in human serum samples.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescence nanohybrid composite CDs@SiO2@QDs for EP and NE was designed and prepared by coating the as-prepared CDs@SiO2 with CdTe QDs on the outer surface via a carbodiimide-mediated approach. The nanoprobe possessed dual emission peaks at 460 and 610 nm under a single wavelength excitation. Under the exposure to EP or NE, only the fluorescence of CdTe QDs can be quenched while the fluorescence of CDs embedded in SiO2 was stable, which leads to the ratiometric fluorescence response to EP and NE. In this detection platform, the brightness and stability of the fluorescence signal of CDs were protected by silica microspheres. Moreover, the silica microspheresas have been used as a carrier loaded with a large number of CdTe quantum dots as signal labels on the surface. The results of the assay of the structural analogues selectivity have revealed that this ratiometric fluorescence detection platform has lowered the threshold for detecting EP and NE and thus improved the sensibility.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21505036) and the Foundation of the Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology (Hubei Normal University, No. PA20170105).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identification of catecholamine neurotransmitters using fluorescence sensor array

Simultaneous determination of plasma epinephrine and norepinephrine using an integrated strategy of a fully automated protein precipitation technique, reductive ethylation labeling and UPLC–MS/MS
Silver nanoprisms-based Tb(III) fluorescence sensor for highly selective detection of dopamine
Colorimetric fingerprints of gold nanorods for discriminating catecholamine neurotransmitters in urine samples
Real-time tracing the changes in the intracellular pH value during apoptosis by near-infrared ratiometric fluorescence imaging
Ratiometric fluorescence, electrochemiluminescence, and photoelectrochemical chemo/biosensing based on semiconductor quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence and mesoporous structure dual signal amplification for sensitive and selective detection of TNT based on MIP@QD fluorescence sensors
Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
A review on syntheses, properties, characterization and bioanalytical applications of fluorescent carbon dots
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Carbon-dot-based Ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging and biosensing of superoxide anion in live cells
Carbon dots with continuously tunable full-color emission and their application in ratiometric pH sensing
Ratiometric phosphorescent probe for thallium in serum, water, and soil samples based on long-lived, spectrally resolved, Mn-doped ZnSe quantum dots and carbon dots
Proteolytic activity monitored by fluorescence resonance energy transfer through quantum-dot−peptide conjugates
Semicondutor quantum dots-based metal ion probes
Application of semiconductor quantum dots in bioimaging and biosensing
Role of a cationic surfactant in mediating interaction of flavonoids with 3-mercaptopropanoic acid capped CdTe quantum dots (3MPA-CdTe QDs)
Aqueous synthesis of CdTe quantum dots by hydride generation for visual detection of silver on quantum dot immobilized paper
Heparin-MPA dual modified CdS quantum dots used as a simple and rapid label-free fluorescent sensor for protamine and hemin detection
Molecularly imprinted polymer based on CdTe@SiO2 quantum dots as a fluorescent sensor for the recognition of norepinephrine
Highly luminescent organosilane functionalized carbon dots
Efficient one-pot synthesis of molecularly imprinted silica nanospheres embedded carbon dots for fluorescent dopamine optosensing
Ratiometric fluorescence biosensor based on CdTe quantum and carbon dots for double strand DNA detection
Ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for selective and visual detection of cadmium ions using quencher displacement-induced fluorescence recovery of CdTe quantum dots-based hybrid probe
An efficient ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on metal-organic frameworks and quantum dots for highly selective detection of 6‑mercaptopurine
Are conformational changes, induced by osmotic pressure variations, the underlying mechanism of controlling the adhesive activity of mussel adhesive proteins?
A wide-color-varying ratiometric nanoprobe for detection of norepinephrine in urine samples
Ultra-selective detection of Fe2+ ion by redox mechanism based on fluorescent polymerized dopamine derivatives
Electrochemical sensor for simultaneous determination of epinephrine and norepinephrine based on cetyltrimethylammonium bromide assisted SnO2 nanoparticles
Ultrasensitive measurement of endogenous epinephrine and norepinephrine in human plasma by semi-automated SPE-LC–MS/MS
Oxidative self-polymerization of dopamine in acidic environment
Molecularly imprinted polymers on dual-color quantum dots for simultaneous detection of norepinephrine and epinephrine
